FlashQL

FlashQL Overview

Federation & Sync

Sync

Conflict Handling

Sync Integration Patterns

Language Reference

Engineering

The Realtime Engine

On this page

Federation, Materialization, Realtime Views, and Write Paths ​

FlashQL lets you treat local and origin data as one relational graph while still controlling:

  • what stays runtime-only
  • what gets cached locally
  • what stays continuously synchronized
  • and how writes made against local views travel back to the origin

This entire system is built around views.

Start with the mental model ​

In FlashQL, a view is not only a query convenience.

For federation and sync, a view becomes the contract between:

  • your local app-facing database surface
  • and some origin relation that may live elsewhere

That means one view definition can answer all of these questions at once:

  • where does this data come from?
  • should it stay runtime-only, be materialized, or stay realtime?
  • can I write to it locally?
  • if I can write to it, how should local state behave before the origin acknowledges?

So this page is really about one thing:

how a local relational view becomes the boundary between your app and an origin database

The two ingredients ​

Two things work together to form the foreign I/O system:

  1. a local database view that points to an origin relation
  2. an upstream client instance that knows how to talk to that origin

At a high level:

  1. the view defines how origin data should appear locally
  2. the upstream client lets FlashQL talk to the origin behind that view

Ordinary local views first ​

Start with the ordinary database idea:

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE VIEW public.posts AS
  SELECT title, content FROM public.blog
`);

Above, querying public.posts resolves against public.blog in the local database.

That is just a normal view.

Turning a view into an origin-backed view ​

FlashQL extends that idea so the same view surface can resolve from an origin outside the local database.

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE VIEW public.posts AS
  SELECT title, content FROM public.blog
  WITH (replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
`);

Now the local view can be read as:

js
await db.query(`
  SELECT * FROM public.posts;
`);

and still participate in local relational work such as joins:

js
await db.query(`
  SELECT *
  FROM public.posts
  LEFT JOIN public.users ON posts.user_id = users.id;
`);

The query executes as one relational graph, but composed of both local and origin data.

The upstream client factory ​

To talk to an origin, FlashQL needs an upstream client instance.

That is provided through getUpstreamClient():

js
import { FlashQL } from '@linked-db/linked-ql/flashql';
import { EdgeClient } from '@linked-db/linked-ql/edge';

const db = new FlashQL({
  async getUpstreamClient(originId) {
    if (originId !== 'postgres:primary') {
      throw new Error(`Unknown origin: ${originId}`);
    }

    return new EdgeClient({
      url: '/api/db',
      dialect: 'postgres',
    });
  },
});

await db.connect();

This is now a local FlashQL instance that can resolve origin-backed views on demand.

The important idea is that replication_origin does not have to be the network URL itself. It is the origin id that your app uses to choose the right upstream client.

replication_origin ​

replication_origin is the switch that makes a view origin-backed.

If it is absent or NULL, the view behaves like an ordinary local view.

If it is present, FlashQL resolves the view through an upstream client.

The current contract ​

At the view level, replication_origin is expected to be one of:

  • a dialect-prefixed origin id such as postgres:primary or flashql:primary
  • INHERIT, meaning "use the schema-level default origin"
  • NULL, meaning "this view is not origin-backed"

Examples:

sql
WITH (replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
sql
WITH (replication_origin = INHERIT)
sql
WITH (replication_origin = NULL)

The important thing is the dialect prefix. That prefix gives FlashQL enough information to choose the right origin adapter family.

Schema-level default origins ​

If many views in one schema point at the same origin, you do not want to repeat that id everywhere.

FlashQL supports a schema-level default through default_replication_origin:

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE SCHEMA remote
  WITH (default_replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
`);

You can also alter it later:

js
await db.query(`
  ALTER SCHEMA remote
  SET (default_replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
`);

Then individual views can inherit that origin:

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE VIEW remote.posts AS
  SELECT * FROM public.posts
  WITH (replication_origin = INHERIT)
`);

So:

  • default_replication_origin is the schema-level default
  • replication_origin = INHERIT is the view-level inheritance hook

Replication modes ​

A view's replication mode decides how origin data should behave locally.

Values can be:

  • none
  • materialized
  • realtime

Runtime views ​

Runtime views are the default.

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE VIEW public.users AS
  SELECT * FROM public.users
  WITH (replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
`);

Behavior:

  • the view is queryable locally as public.users
  • each read resolves through the upstream client at runtime
  • rows are not copied into local table storage

Use this when you do not need an offline copy.

materialized views ​

materialized views pull origin rows into local storage:

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW public.users AS
  SELECT * FROM public.users
  WITH (replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
`);

Use this when:

  • offline reads matter
  • local latency matters
  • periodic reconciliation is enough

realtime views ​

realtime views materialize locally and then stay synchronized:

js
await db.query(`
  CREATE REALTIME VIEW public.posts AS
  SELECT * FROM public.posts
  WITH (replication_origin = 'postgres:primary')
`);

Behavior:

  • rows are copied into the local view
  • the view stays subscribed to origin commits
  • inbound origin changes automatically apply to the local copy

Use this when you want local querying and continuous alignment.

Replication options ​

Besides replication_origin and replication_mode, FlashQL supports additional replication options.

These fall into two groups:

  • query-planning options for federated runtime execution
  • conflict and outbound-write options for replicated writable views

Join-related options ​

These options affect how FlashQL plans federated join work:

  • join_pushdown_size
  • join_memoization

Example:

sql
CREATE VIEW public.posts AS
SELECT * FROM public.blog
WITH (
  replication_origin = 'postgres:primary',
  join_pushdown_size = 10,
  join_memoization = TRUE
);

Practical meaning:

  • join_pushdown_size controls how aggressively FlashQL should push join work toward the origin side when federated execution is possible
  • join_memoization controls whether equivalent join work should be memoized to avoid repeating fetches

These matter most for runtime federation, where reads are resolved on demand across local and origin relations.

Writable origin-backed views ​

FlashQL also extends updateable-view semantics so that writes against a local view can become writes against the origin relation.

The important idea is:

  • you write to the view
  • FlashQL derives how that view maps back to the origin relation
  • the view's write policy decides what local state should do before the origin acknowledges

This works best for direct and analyzable projection views.

Write policies ​

Replicated views support two write policies:

  • origin_first
  • local_first

origin_first ​

  • local writes queue outbound work
  • the visible local row does not change inline
  • inbound origin commits later materialize the authoritative row state

local_first ​

  • local writes stage row changes immediately
  • rows carry __staged = true until authoritative inbound arrives
  • outbound work is still queued and sent in the background

In both models, inbound origin commits remain the authoritative source of row state.

The __staged system column ​

FlashQL exposes a predictable __staged boolean on synced views.

Its meaning is:

  • false this row is authoritative locally
  • true this row was born or changed locally and is still staged

upstream_mvcc_key ​

For replicated writable views, the other important knob is upstream_mvcc_key.

Example:

sql
CREATE REALTIME VIEW public.users AS
SELECT * FROM public.users
WITH (
  replication_origin = 'postgres:primary',
  upstream_mvcc_key = 'XMIN'
);

This tells FlashQL which origin-side version token should be used to condition outbound writes.

That is what makes conflict handling predictable instead of silent last-writer-wins overwrites.

For PostgreSQL- and FlashQL-backed origins, FlashQL can often default this to XMIN.

Conflict expectations ​

Conflict handling is part of the view definition story, not an afterthought.

That is why replicated writable views can carry:

  • write_policy
  • upstream_mvcc_key

Those options let FlashQL do two things:

  • decide what the user sees locally before origin acknowledgment
  • decide how outbound writes should be conditionally applied at the origin

The practical result is predictable conflict behavior rather than silent overwrites.

The full version-token and queue-state story is covered in:

  • Conflict Handling

Writeability expectations ​

FlashQL's writable views are recognizable as updateable views, but they are not claiming to implement every possible SQL-standard or trigger-driven updatable-view pattern.

Today, the strongest writeable and syncable shapes are:

  • direct relation references
  • simple analyzable projections over one writable source relation

And some valid view definitions are intentionally non-updatable for replicated write paths, especially:

  • UNION-backed views
  • VALUES-backed views
  • more complex CTE-driven shapes

Putting it together ​

So the full foreign I/O story is:

  • replication_origin decides whether the view is local or origin-backed
  • replication_mode decides whether origin data stays runtime-only, cached, or continuously synced
  • join-related replication options tune federated runtime execution
  • write-related replication options define local write visibility and conflict behavior

That is why views are the center of FlashQL federation and sync rather than just one piece of it.

Related docs ​

  • FlashQL Sync
  • Conflict Handling
  • Sync Integration Patterns

Appendix A: join_* options ​

The join_* options are runtime-federation controls.

They are relevant when a view is being resolved on demand across local and origin relations. In other words, they are runtime-view concerns.

The main ones are:

  • join_pushdown_size
  • join_memoization

join_pushdown_size ​

This controls how aggressively FlashQL should push join work toward the origin side when federated execution is possible.

Higher values generally mean:

  • more origin-side work can be attempted
  • fewer round trips may be needed

Lower values generally mean:

  • FlashQL keeps more of the join work local
  • planning stays conservative

join_memoization ​

This controls whether repeated federated join work should be memoized.

That can help when:

  • the same local-to-origin join work repeats
  • the same lookup pattern would otherwise refetch equivalent origin subsets

Important scope note ​

These options are about federated runtime reads.

They are not the knobs that define:

  • materialization
  • realtime synchronization
  • outbound write semantics
  • conflict detection

Those belong to the sync and writable-view story instead.

Appendix B: MVCC in the sync story ​

The conflict story for FlashQL sync leans on an origin-side version token.

That is what upstream_mvcc_key configures.

Why PostgreSQL and FlashQL fit well here ​

Both PostgreSQL and FlashQL have MVCC-oriented row versioning stories.

In practice, that gives FlashQL a natural token to use when it needs to say:

"apply this write only if the row is still the version I saw"

For PostgreSQL and FlashQL origins, that token is often XMIN.

What FlashQL does with that token ​

For a replicated writable view, FlashQL can:

  1. project the origin version token into local row state
  2. carry that token with the queued outbound write
  3. apply the outbound write conditionally at the origin

If the origin row version no longer matches, the write can be classified as a conflict instead of silently overwriting newer state.

Why upstream_mvcc_key exists ​

Not every origin exposes the same version token shape.

That is why the view-level upstream_mvcc_key option exists:

  • it makes the token configurable
  • it lets the same conflict model adapt to different origin systems

For the full conflict story, see Conflict Handling.
